blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ (١)
Say, ‘He is Allah, the One. (1)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ (٢)
Allah is the All-embracing. (2)
لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (٣)
He neither begat, nor was begotten, (3)
وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ (٤)
nor has He any equal.’ (4)

(Commentary)

The Surah describes the Exalted as possessing the oneness of essence (أحدية الذات) and as the One to Whom everything other than Him returns for all its existential needs, without anything sharing with Him in His essence, His attributes, or His acts. This is the Quranic monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — which is unique to the Holy Quran and upon which all Islamic sciences are built.

Narrations regarding the merit of this Surah have been numerous, to the extent that it has been reported through the chains of both groups (the Sunnis and Shias) that it is equivalent to one-third of the Quran, as will be mentioned later, Allah willing.

The Surah could potentially be either Meccan or Medinan, though it appears from some reports concerning the occasion of its revelation (شأن نزولها) that it is Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ Say, ‘He is Allah, the One. (1) The word "He" (هُوَ) is the pronoun of the matter and the story (ضمير الشأن والقصة), which serves to emphasize the importance of the content of the sentence following it. The truth is that the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) "Allah" is a proper name (علم) by predominant usage for the Exalted in Arabic, just as He has a specific name in other languages. Some discussion regarding this has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-Fatiha.

The word "One" (أحد) is an attribute derived from unity (الوحدة), like "the Single" (الواحد), except that "the One" (الأحد) is only applied to that which accepts no plurality (الكثرة), neither externally (خارجا) nor mentally (ذهنا). Therefore, it does not accept counting nor does it enter into number, unlike "the Single" (الواحد), for every "single" thing has a second and a third, either externally or mentally through imagination or intellectual assumption, becoming many by such addition. As for "the One" (الأحد), whatever second is assumed for it, it remains itself; nothing is added to it.

Consider this in your saying: "No one (أحد) from the people came to me." By this, you negate the coming of two or more of them just as you negate the coming of one. This is unlike saying: "One (واحد) of them did not come to me," for you are only negating the coming of one by number, which does not contradict the coming of two or more. Because it conveys this meaning, it is never used in an affirmative sense (الإيجاب) except for the Exalted. Among the subtle expressions in this regard is the saying of Ali, upon him be the best of peace (عليه أفضل السلام), in one of his sermons regarding His monotheism (توحيده): "Everything named with unity (الوحدة) other than Him is small." We have cited a portion of his speech (peace be upon him) regarding monotheism (التوحيد) at the end of the discussion on the monotheism of the Quran in the sixth volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ الصَّمَدُ Allah is the All-embracing. (2) The linguistic root of the meaning of al-Samad (الصمد) is "intent" (القصد) or "intent accompanied by reliance." It is said: "He intended him" (صمده يصمده صمدا) from the paradigm of nasara, meaning he sought him or sought him while relying upon him. Lexicographers have interpreted al-Samad—which is an attribute—with various meanings, most of which return to the idea that He is the Master to Whom all resort (المصمود إليه), meaning the One intended for needs. Since it is used in the verse without any restriction, He is the One intended for needs in an absolute sense.

Since Allah the Exalted is the Originator of every possessor of existence other than Him, everything that can be called a "thing" besides Him is in need of Him and thus intends Him in its essence, its attributes, and its effects. The Exalted said: أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ Look! To Him belong the creation and the command. (7:54), and He said in an absolute sense: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى and that the terminus is toward your Lord, (53:42). Thus, He is the All-embracing (الصمد) in every need in existence; no intent is directed toward anything except that He is the One where that intent ends, through Whom the request is successful, and by Whom the need is fulfilled.

From here, the reason for the inclusion of the definite article (اللام) in al-Samad (الصمد) becomes clear: it is to signify exclusivity (الحصر). Thus, He the Exalted alone is the All-embracing in the absolute sense. This is in contrast to "One" (أحد) in His saying: اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Allah is One), for "One" (أحد), with the specific meaning of unity it conveys, is not applied in the affirmative to anyone other than the Exalted; thus, there is no need for the definite article or exclusivity there.

As for the explicit mention of the Majestic Name a second time where it is said: اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah is the All-embracing), rather than saying "He is the All-embracing" or "Allah is One, All-embracing," it appears that this is to indicate that each of the two sentences is independently sufficient in defining the Exalted. Since the context is one of defining the Exalted by an attribute unique to Him, it was said: "Allah is One, Allah is the All-embracing," indicating that knowledge of Him is attained whether one says this or that.

Furthermore, the two verses describe the Exalted with both an attribute of essence (صفة الذات) and an attribute of act (صفة الفعل). His saying: اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Allah is One) describes Him with oneness (الأحدية), which is identical to the Essence (عين الذات). His saying: اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah is the All-embracing) describes Him as the ultimate point to which everything returns, which is among the attributes of act.

It has also been said that al-Samad means the Solid (المصمت) who is not hollow, thus He does not eat, drink, sleep, beget, nor is He begotten. According to this, His saying: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ (He neither begat nor was begotten) would be an explanation of al-Samad.

His saying, the Exalted: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ He neither begat nor was begotten, nor has He any equal.’ (3-4) These two noble verses negate that He the Exalted should beget anything by undergoing division within Himself, such that something of His own kind (سنخه) separates from Him, in whatever sense separation and derivation (الاشتقاق) are intended—as the Christians claim regarding the Messiah (peace be upon him) that he is the son of Allah, and as the pagans claim regarding some of their deities that they are the children of Allah, glorified is He.

They also negate that He should be begotten from another thing or derived from it in whatever sense derivation is intended—as the pagans claim, for among their deities are those who are a god-father of a god, a goddess-mother of a god, or a god-son of a god.

They further negate that He should have any equal (كفؤ) who matches Him in His essence or in His act (1), which is creation and governance (التدبير). None of the followers of religions or others have claimed an essential equal (الكفؤ الذاتي) by asserting a plurality of the Necessary Being (واجب الوجود), exalted is His Name. As for an equal in His act, which is governance, this has been claimed, such as the pagan deities among humans like Pharaoh and Nimrod who claimed divinity. The basis of equality for them was the independence (استقلال) of the one they viewed as divine in governing what was entrusted to him, just as He the Exalted is independent in governing those He governs; they were the lords and gods, and He was the Lord of lords and God of gods.

Within the meaning of equality of this type of deity is what is assumed of the independence of action in any of the contingent beings (الممكنات); for that is an equality whose reality is its self-sufficiency from the Exalted, while it is in fact needy from every direction. The verse negates this.

These three negated attributes, although their negation can be derived from the attribute of His oneness (أحدية) in a certain way, the most immediate thought is their derivation from the attribute of His being the All-embracing (صمدية).

As for Him not begetting, begetting—which is a type of division and part-taking (التبعض) in whatever way it is interpreted—is not free from composition (تركيب) in the one who begets. The need of a composite being for its parts is necessary, while Allah, glorified is He, is the All-embracing (صمد) to Whom every needy one turns for its need, while He has no need. As for Him not being begotten, the begetting of one thing from another cannot occur except with the need of the begotten for that from which it was begotten for its existence, while He, glorified is He, is the All-embracing (صمد) who has no need. As for Him having no equal, it is because an equal—whether assumed as an equal in His essence or in His act—cannot have its equality realized except with its independence and self-sufficiency from the Exalted in that which constitutes the equality, while Allah, glorified is He, is the All-embracing (صمد) in the absolute sense, upon Whom everyone other than Him is dependent from every assumed direction.

Thus, it has become clear that the negation in the two verses is derived from His being the All-embracing (صمدية), and the sum of what has been mentioned regarding His being the All-embracing and what follows from it leads to the affirmation of His uniqueness (توحد) in His essence, attributes, and acts. This means He is One (واحد) whom nothing rivals and nothing resembles; His essence is by His essence and for His essence, without reliance upon another or need for anyone else. The same applies to His attributes and acts. The essences of those other than Him, their attributes, and their acts exist by an outpouring (إفاضة) from Him in a manner befitting the presence of His majesty and greatness. The summary of the Surah is the description of the Exalted as being One (أحد) and Single (واحد).

(1) We did not mention the attribute because it is either an attribute of essence, which is identical to the essence, or an attribute of act, which is abstracted from the act. — From the author.

Among what has been said regarding the verse is that "equal" (الكفؤ) refers to a spouse, for a man's wife is his equal; thus, it would be in the sense of His saying: تَعالى جَدُّ رَبِّنا مَا اتَّخَذَ صاحِبَةً Exalted be the majesty of our Lord; He has taken neither any spouse (72:3). This is as you see (i.e., a weak interpretation).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Jews asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Attribute your Lord to us.' He remained for three days without answering them, then قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ (Say, ‘He is Allah, the One’) was revealed to the end."

I say: In al-Ihtijaj, from Imam al-Askari (peace be upon him): "The questioner was Abdullah ibn Suriya the Jew." In some Sunni narrations: "The questioner was Abdullah ibn Salam who asked him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that in Mecca, then he believed and concealed his faith." In some of them: "A group of Jews asked him that." In more than one of their narrations: "The polytheists of Mecca asked him that." In any case, what is meant by "attribution" (النسبة) is the description and characterization.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain to al-Asbagh ibn Nubata, from Ali (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "The attribution (نسبة) of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is 'Say, He is Allah...'"

In al-Ilal, with its chain to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in the tradition of the Ascension (المعراج): "Allah said to him—meaning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'Recite قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ as it was revealed, for it is My attribution (نسبتي) and My description.'"

I say: It is also narrated with its chain to Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) with a similar meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Ubayd narrated in his Fada'il from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ is one-third of the Quran."

I say: Narrations from their chains have been numerous in this regard; they narrated it from several companions such as Ibn Abbas—as mentioned—Abu al-Darda, Ibn Umar, Jabir, Ibn Mas'ud, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Mu'adh ibn Anas, Abu Ayyub, Abu Umama, and others from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It has also been reported in several narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). They have explained the Surah being equivalent to one-third of the Quran in various ways, the most balanced of which is that the knowledge in the Quran resolves into three principles: monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the hereafter (المعاد), and the Surah contains one of the three, which is monotheism.

In al-Tawhid, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "I saw al-Khidr (peace be upon him) in a dream the night before Badr. I said to him: 'Teach me something by which I may be victorious over the enemies.' He said: 'Say: O He, O One besides Whom there is no "He" (يا هو يا من لا هو إلا هو).' When morning came, I recounted it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said to me: 'O Ali, you have been taught the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم).' So it was on my tongue on the day of Badr."

And the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) recited قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ, and when he finished, he said: "O He, O One besides Whom there is no 'He', forgive me and help me against the faithless people."

In Nahj al-Balagha: "The One (الأحد) not by the interpretation of number."

I say: It is narrated in al-Tawhid from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) with the wording: "One (أحد) not by the interpretation of number."

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain to Dawud ibn al-Qasim al-Ja'fari, who said: "I said to Abu Ja'far the Second (peace be upon him): 'What is the All-embracing (الصمد)?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'The Master to Whom all resort (السيد المصمود إليه) in the small and the great.'"

I say: Regarding the interpretation of al-Samad, other meanings are narrated from them (peace be upon them). From Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "al-Samad is the obeyed Master above whom there is no commander or forbidder." From Imam al-Husayn (peace be upon him): "al-Samad is He who has no hollow, al-Samad is He who does not sleep, al-Samad is He who has always been and will always be." From Imam al-Sajjad (peace be upon him): "al-Samad is He who, when He intends a thing, says to it 'Be' and it is, and al-Samad is He who originated things—creating them as opposites, forms, and pairs—and remained unique in oneness without opposite, form, likeness, or peer."

The linguistic root of the meaning of al-Samad is what we narrated from Abu Ja'far the Second (peace be upon him), due to what its root signifies linguistically in terms of "intent." The various meanings reported from them (peace be upon them) in interpretation are inseparable from this meaning, for the mentioned meanings are necessary concomitants of His being the One intended to Whom everything returns in every need; thus, everything ends with Him without any need being realized within Him.

In al-Tawhid, from Wahb ibn Wahb al-Qurashi from Imam al-Sadiq from his fathers (peace be upon them), that the people of Basra wrote to al-Husayn ibn Ali (peace be upon him) asking him about al-Samad. He wrote to them: "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. To proceed: Do not plunge into the Quran, do not dispute regarding it, and do not speak about it without knowledge. For I heard my grandfather the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Whoever speaks regarding the Quran without knowledge, let him take his seat in the Fire.' Indeed, Allah, glorified is He, interpreted al-Samad, for He said: اللهُ أَحَدٌ اللهُ الصَّمَدُ (Allah is One, Allah is the All-embracing), then He interpreted it saying: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ كُفُواً أَحَدٌ (He neither begat nor was begotten, nor has He any equal)."

In the same source, with its chain to Ibn Abi Umayr from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said: "Know that Allah the Exalted is One, Single, All-embracing—He did not beget so as to be inherited, nor was He begotten so as to be shared."

In the same source, in another sermon by Ali (peace be upon him): "He who was not begotten so as to be shared in glory, nor did He beget so as to be a perishing inherited one."

In the same source, in a sermon of his (peace be upon him): "Exalted is He above having an equal such that He would be resembled by it."

I say: There are other narrations regarding the preceding meanings.
